rationale_for_baseline_tier: Uncomplicated acute lower urinary tract infection in a non-pregnant adult woman with no systemic or upper-tract features (no fever, rigors, flank pain or vomiting) is a common, low-acuity, telehealth/GP/pharmacist-manageable condition. It warrants prompt access to a first-line oral antibiotic within roughly 24–48 hours (T2) plus symptomatic advice and a clear pyelonephritis safety-net — NOT emergency care. The source note's 'urgent after-hours home visit' framing reflects an in-person billing context (MBS 585-600), not true clinical acuity; escalating this to an emergency department or ambulance would be an over-triage. Antibiotic access is time-sensitive enough to prefer T2 over routine 1–2 week review, given the patient's severe symptoms and distress.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe back or flank (loin) pain, high fever, or shaking chills (rigors)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department if these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate ascending pyelonephritis, which needs urgent assessment and possibly intravenous antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop bad pain in your back or side, a high fever, or shaking chills, go to an emergency department the same day — the infection may have reached your kidney.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting or being unable to keep the antibiotic/fluids down
escalation_edges.rationale: Inability to tolerate oral therapy means oral antibiotics will fail and IV treatment/rehydration is needed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start vomiting and can't keep the tablets or fluids down, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Visible blood in the urine that does not clear after the first few doses of antibiotics
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent visible haematuria makes the UTI complicated and may need culture, a different antibiotic, or investigation of an alternative cause.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see blood in your urine that doesn't clear after the first few doses, see your GP within a day or two.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement after 48 hours of antibiotics, or symptoms worsening
escalation_edges.rationale: Treatment failure suggests resistance or an alternative diagnosis; a urine culture and antibiotic review are needed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you're not improving after two days on the antibiotics, or you're getting worse, see your GP to review the treatment and send a urine sample.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling gravely unwell — confusion, drowsiness, high fever with rigors, fainting or very rapid heartbeat (features of sepsis)
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of urosepsis, a life-threatening emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become confused or very drowsy, get a high fever with violent shaking, or feel like you might pass out, call 000 — this could be a serious blood infection.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Pregnancy status not stated in the source; sex inferred female
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: If the patient is pregnant, this is no longer an uncomplicated UTI: it needs urine culture, a pregnancy-safe antibiotic (avoid trimethoprim in the first trimester and nitrofurantoin at term) and closer follow-up. Confirm pregnancy status before applying the uncomplicated plan.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a bladder infection that can be treated with a short course of antibiotics arranged through a telehealth prescription, your GP, or a pharmacist UTI service within the next day. Start the antibiotic, drink plenty of water, and use simple pain relief such as paracetamol. You do not need an emergency department for this on its own.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Telehealth prescription or GP/pharmacist within 24–48 hours; emergency department only if red-flag features develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and you cannot get a prescription tonight, simple pain relief and fluids are safe to start now, and you can arrange the antibiotic in the morning — unless any of the emergency warning signs below appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP with the symptom onset and course, the pyelonephritis red-flag screen (negative), the antibiotic started, and advise a urine culture if symptoms persist or recur.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the patient or run a dipstick, so the diagnosis is presumptive and pyelonephritis is excluded on history alone.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat empirically as an uncomplicated lower UTI after a careful red-flag screen, and safety-net explicitly for the features that would change the picture (fever, flank pain, vomiting, systemic illness).
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing the symptoms without arranging antibiotic access and safety-netting, OR over-reacting to diagnostic uncertainty by sending a well, systemically-stable patient to the emergency department.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the symptoms as trivial self-care with no antibiotic access and no safety-net, missing an evolving or already-present pyelonephritis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised ascending infection progresses to pyelonephritis and urosepsis.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a routine 1–2 week GP appointment despite severe symptoms, delaying antibiotic access, risking prolonged symptoms and infection ascent before treatment starts.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically well patient with an uncomplicated lower UTI to an emergency department or calling an ambulance — unnecessary ED burden, cost, delay, patient anxiety and nosocomial exposure for a community-manageable condition.
